topic: Eclipse magnetic ensemble (39 eclipses) — post-c12 patches
our_argument_summary: WIN-068 cites Kim & Chang (2018) 39-eclipse INTERMAGNET ensemble (1991–2016). Our verdict: Std Model Explains — Chapman Sq ionospheric framework plus Kim & Chang ensemble predict the signal magnitude, geometry, and latitude dependence without any dome-derived parameter. WIN-068 also inflates the score by triple-counting the same phenomenon (alongside WIN-010 BOU 2017 and WIN-025 2024 9-station). FSF scaling validation is cross-referenced to WIN-010/025 where it actually enters the predicted signal. Post-hoc critique: Kim & Chang 2018 data is 8 years older than V51.0 incorporation.

dome_code_analysis.calculation_pipeline: No WIN-068 domain in monitor.py. The 39-eclipse ensemble is a retrospective literature citation — no live validation logic, no API call, no comparison against a dome-derived numerical prediction.
dome_code_analysis.hardcoded_vs_computed: monitoring = none. The WIN entry exists in the published list but the automation does not touch it.
dome_code_analysis.escape_clauses: N/A — no validation logic to escape from.
dome_code_analysis.code_discrepancies: None. Our claim that monitor.py has no validation for WIN-068 is accurate; grep for 068 / eclipse / 39-eclipse in monitor.py returns no signal-amplitude comparison code.

code_analysis_tags.monitoring_evidence: No 39-eclipse ensemble domain block in monitor.py. Eclipse-related code targets the 2026 forward projection (WIN-010 lineage), not the 1991–2016 retrospective ensemble that WIN-068 actually counts.
code_analysis_tags.relabels_standard_evidence: The phenomenon (eclipse-induced Y-component increase, X/Z/F decrease, 30–50° magnetic-latitude peak, ~180-minute window) is explained by the Chapman–Sq framework (Chapman 1919 Phil. Trans. A 218; Rastogi 1962 eclipse-specific; Yamazaki & Maute 2021 modern conductivity). Dome renames cause (cavity geometry) without changing the numerical prediction.
code_analysis_tags.post_hoc_evidence: Kim & Chang (2018, Adv. Space Res. 61:2040–2054) published 8 years before V51.0 incorporation (early 2026). No pre-registration of expected values in a venue independent of the author could be located.
code_analysis_tags.derives_from_dome_evidence: The ensemble signal amplitude is extracted from the 1991–2016 INTERMAGNET dataset itself. FSF (which IS dome geometry per the c12 retraction) does not enter this WIN — it is a spectator on the 39-eclipse ensemble and only relevant to the WIN-010/025 2026 forward projection.

holes_found.description: Intra-WIN Chapman-year inconsistency introduced by the c12 Finding-only patch. The Finding now correctly cites "Chapman 1919, with eclipse-specific application in Rastogi 1962 and modern conductivity treatment in Yamazaki & Maute 2021". But detail_evidence ¶3 still reads "Chapman (1933, Terrestrial Magnetism and Atmospheric Electricity 38:3)", detail_verdict_text ¶1 still reads "Chapman (1933, Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. 38:3)", AND tldr_verdict still reads "The Chapman mechanism (documented since 1933 ...)". A reader scanning the WIN-068 panel sees Chapman-1919 in the summary row Finding, Chapman-1933 in the collapsed TLDR verdict, and Chapman-1933 in both expanded detail blocks. The c12 patch narrowed the mis-specialization from "wrong year everywhere" to "right year in one place, wrong in three others" — and the inconsistency is now visible within a single WIN. Defender wedge: "the review team can't keep its own Chapman citation straight across the same WIN panel." ISS-1491 was opened for this but its description still says "Finding field says Chapman 1933" — it was drafted against pre-805cd55 state and is now mis-scoped; the bug it describes moved rather than closed.
holes_found.recommendation: Propagate the Finding's Chapman 1919 / Rastogi 1962 / Yamazaki & Maute 2021 attribution to detail_evidence ¶3, detail_verdict_text ¶1, and tldr_verdict in a single coordinated patch. Re-scope ISS-1491 to enumerate the three remaining locations (detail_evidence, detail_verdict_text, tldr_verdict) rather than the already-fixed Finding. Coordinate with ISS-1441 (citation format) and ISS-1492 (cross-WIN consistency) — these three issues are the same bug at three scopes (single field → single WIN → cluster).

holes_found.description: tldr_verdict has a separate small imprecision independent of the year issue: "The Chapman mechanism (documented since 1933 and confirmed across 39 eclipses by Kim & Chang 2018) predicts the ensemble signal without any dome parameters." Saying "documented since 1933" is a timeline understatement — the Chapman–Sq framework was established in 1919 and eclipse-specific applications appear throughout the mid-20th century. "Documented since 1933" is not literally false (the mechanism HAD been documented by 1933), but it understates the maturity and gives a defender a minor opening: "the review admits the mechanism is only 93 years old at most, not centuries old." Low-effort fix as part of the year propagation above.
holes_found.recommendation: In tldr_verdict, replace "documented since 1933" with "documented since Chapman (1919)" or "documented across the Chapman–Sq literature". Trivial edit; ride the coordinated patch from the moderate above.

holes_found.description: EXP-241 compression of the FSF scope leak (c12 H1) preserved "Pearson r = 0.9999" and the "retraction of the prior empirical fitting framing" signal inside both detail_evidence ¶3 tail and detail_verdict_text ¶2 before redirecting to WIN-010. This matches the c12 recommendation verbatim — the recommended replacement sentence ALSO kept "Pearson r=0.9999" inline — so the compression is compliant with the plan. A defender-quote-mine risk remains (they can grab "Pearson r = 0.9999" + "retraction" from WIN-068 and present as a concession to THIS WIN), but the redirect bracketing ("— see WIN-010 and WIN-025, where FSF actually enters the predicted signal. FSF is not invoked anywhere in the 39-eclipse Kim & Chang (2018) ensemble evaluated in this WIN.") is explicit enough that any such quote-mine is in bad faith. Noting this for completeness — the c12 H1 finding is superseded by EXP-241 and I am NOT re-opening it.
holes_found.recommendation: No action. The c12 H1 moderate is superseded by EXP-241 (commit 098b1a8 closing ISS-1490). Log this as residual-but-accepted, tracked via the c12 review history.

holes_found.description: Recurrence-pattern concern: the Chapman citation has been the subject of, by my count at this point, ISS-1062, ISS-1286, ISS-1287, ISS-1308, ISS-1441, ISS-1491, ISS-1492 — seven distinct issues for what is fundamentally one citation decision (which Chapman paper anchors the Sq mechanism across the WIN-010/025/068 cluster). The c12 patch fixed one field and spawned a new intra-WIN inconsistency (see hole #1). If the coordinated cluster-wide canonical-Chapman patch keeps getting deferred in favor of single-field edits, we will keep generating new Chapman issues. Meta-finding, not a text hole.
holes_found.recommendation: Recommend the decider escalate ISS-1441/1491/1492 into a single cluster EXP that canonicalizes the Chapman citation across WIN-010, WIN-025, and WIN-068 in one commit, and closes the whole family of related issues together. Piecemeal fixing is generating more issues than it closes.

holes_found.description: verdict_history still shows a 24-hour two-flip sequence (2026-04-12 Std→Self-Contradicted via EXP-078 refraction cascade; 2026-04-13 Self-Contradicted→Std Model Explains restored on curmudgeon gate-2 apparent-vs-physical-shadow analysis). c12 H6 already flagged this; decider accepted it as low-priority auditability noise. Still true in c13; the record is not user-visible and the flips are individually well-reasoned. Not re-opening — carrying forward as known, accepted.
holes_found.recommendation: No action. Consider, at some point, adding a verdict_history.stability_note field explaining "flip resolved on apparent-vs-physical-shadow distinction; stable since 2026-04-13" so auditors do not reconstruct it. Not urgent.

stronger_arguments.description: The Rastogi 1962 citation in the Finding is a family reference rather than a specific paper. Rastogi published eclipse-Sq work in multiple 1962-era venues (including studies of the 15 February 1961 eclipse and the 5 February 1962 Peru electrojet eclipse). If the Finding is going to cite Rastogi 1962 inline, a fuller citation (journal + DOI or page range) would close the defender wedge: "which Rastogi paper? These are not interchangeable observations." Medium-effort citation completeness fix; fold into the canonical Chapman patch if escalated.
sources: Rastogi, R. G. (1962) and subsequent eclipse-Sq literature

stronger_arguments.description: The triple-counting argument (WIN-010 / WIN-025 / WIN-068 = same mechanism at three aggregation levels) would be stronger with explicit cross-reference to a cluster-level score-inflation tally. Currently the three WINs each mention being duplicated, but the dome-side scoreboard still shows three separate green marks. A single aggregation table somewhere in the review (or in Section 11 of the walkthrough) showing "dome's claimed confirmation count → deduplicated count" would make the inflation visible rather than relying on the reader to follow three cross-references.

kernel_of_truth.description: Kim & Chang (2018) really did confirm the systematic 39-eclipse INTERMAGNET pattern with rigorous statistics. The dome is citing real peer-reviewed science, and its narrative description of what happens during an eclipse (moon's shadow → reduced ionization → weakened Sq → measurable magnetic perturbation tracking shadow path) is physically correct. Separately, the FSF = H(r)/r derivation is real dome geometry (Pearson r = 0.9999 against the dome's nine published FSF values) — a legitimate, retracted-from-our-side concession.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Citing a correct observation that standard physics already explains is not a confirmed prediction. The 1991–2016 data was published 8 years before V51.0 incorporation, so this is a retrospective literature citation, not a prospective test. The FSF geometry concession is germane to WIN-010/025 (where FSF enters the 2026 forward projection); it is a spectator on the 39-eclipse ensemble that WIN-068 actually counts, and the 2026 Boulder anchor magnitude is still adopted from the 2017 observation rather than derived from dome parameters.

deeper_analysis:
Post-c12 state of WIN-068 is substantially cleaner than pre-c12. Four of seven c12 holes are either self-applied (H2 tldr_evidence attribution, H4 ISS-1442 duplicate Tier-1 sentence, H5 ISS-1443 Coordinate Collapse terminology) or superseded by EXP-241 (H1 scope leak, ISS-1490 closed in commit 098b1a8). The verdict (Std Model Explains) is sound and well-supported by the text. The recovery from the c12 cycle — narrowed the attack surface, preserved substantive content (FSF retraction signal), added explicit cross-referencing — is a net quality improvement.

The main residual after c13 review is the intra-WIN Chapman-year inconsistency created by patching only the Finding. This is a textbook case of piecemeal editing leaving a worse state than either "fix nothing" or "fix everywhere." The recommended fix (propagate Chapman 1919 + Rastogi 1962 + Yamazaki & Maute 2021 to all four Chapman-mentioning fields) is a single coordinated patch of ~4 short substitutions. Combined with the adjacent cross-WIN consistency fix (WIN-010/025 also use different Chapman citations per ISS-1492), this becomes a small cluster-level canonicalization — escalation to an EXP is justified if the decider would otherwise fix one field at a time and continue generating Chapman issues.

The other side of this argument (per the reviewer's adversarial stance): the Chapman inconsistency is genuinely minor from a scientific accuracy standpoint. The mechanism described is the same mechanism regardless of which Chapman paper is cited; the 1919 Phil. Trans. A work is foundational and the 1933 Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. work, while about lunar tides rather than solar-eclipse Sq, does reference the same underlying physical framework. A defender who actually engages with the physics will not gain ground by pointing at citation-format drift. The optics issue is entirely about an inattentive skim-read, and the fix is cheap, but escalating this to a moderate severity may overstate the real risk to the review's credibility. Keeping at moderate because three-location inconsistency across summary / TLDR / detail is qualitatively worse than the pre-c12 state of uniform wrongness — but I note the severity is on the upper edge of what the finding warrants.

citations_verified: Kim & Chang (2018) Adv. Space Res. 61:2040–2054 — real paper, correctly attributed
Yamazaki & Maute (2021) Front. Astron. Space Sci. DOI 10.3389/fspas.2021.766327 — real paper and open-access URL verifiable (flagged in ISS-1062/1286 for DOI recheck but content attribution accurate)
Chapman (1919) Phil. Trans. A 218:1–118 — the canonical Sq-theory paper (cited in Finding, post-805cd55)
citations_failed: Chapman (1933) Terr. Mag. Atmos. Electr. 38:3 — real paper but describes LUNAR geomagnetic tides, not solar-eclipse ionospheric Sq. Still inline in detail_evidence ¶3, detail_verdict_text ¶1, and implicitly in tldr_verdict "documented since 1933". Mis-attribution to the eclipse-Sq mechanism.
Rastogi (1962) — inline in Finding without full venue/DOI; Rastogi published eclipse-Sq work in multiple 1962 venues, so the citation is ambiguous until completed.
citations_unchecked: r·tan(θ) = 8537·exp(−r/8619) — the dome-coordinate equation used to recompute FSF. Not a citation per se; cross-referenced to WIN-010.
V51.0 release date ("early 2026") — used to establish the 8-year post-hoc gap. Not externally verified in this run; carried over from prior cycles.

change_summary: Post-c12 self-apply (commit 805cd55) + EXP-241 integration (commit 098b1a8). c12 H0 scope-leak fixed via EXP-241 cross-reference compression. c12 H2/H4/H5 fixed by self-apply. c12 H1 Finding fix created new intra-WIN Chapman-year inconsistency (Finding 1919, detail blocks + tldr_verdict 1933). c12 H3/H6 unchanged.
